request.question: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?

title: Player's Handbook
Blackmoor (supplement)
Dungeons & Dragons (1974)
Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set
Dungeons & Dragons
Greyhawk (supplement)
Paranoia (role-playing game)
Gary Gygax
Don't Give Up the Ship!
Greyhawk
sentences: The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").
It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.
Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".
Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Blackmoor is a supplementary rulebook (product designation TSR 2004) of the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game written by Dave Arneson (with a foreword by Gary Gygax).
The original Dungeons & Dragons (commonly abbreviated "D&D") boxed set by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson was published by TSR, Inc. in 1974.
It initially included the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
Its product designation was TSR 2002.
The Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set is a set of rulebooks for the "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") fantasy role-playing game.
First published in 1977, it saw a handful of revisions and reprintings.
The first edition was written by J. Eric Holmes based on Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson's original work.
Later editions were edited by Tom Moldvay, Dave Cook, and Frank Mentzer.

Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).
The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.
It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.
"D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.
Greyhawk is a supplementary rulebook written by Gary Gygax and Robert J. Kuntz for the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
It has been called "the first and most important supplement" to the original "D&D" rules.
By adding a combat system, it severed all ties with "Chainmail", making "D&D" a truly stand-alone game system.

Although the name of the book was taken from the home campaign supervised by Gygax and Kuntz based on Gygax's imagined Castle Greyhawk and the lands surrounding it, "Greyhawk" did not give any details of the castle or the campaign world; instead, it explained the rules that Gygax and Kuntz used in their home campaign, and introduced a number of character classes, spells, concepts and monsters used in all subsequent editions of "D&D".
Paranoia is a dystopian science-fiction tabletop role-playing game originally designed and written by Greg Costikyan, Dan Gelber, and Eric Goldberg, and first published in 1984 by West End Games.
Since 2004 the game has been published under license by Mongoose Publishing.
The game won the Origins Award for Best Roleplaying Rules of 1984 and was inducted into the Origins Awards Hall of Fame in 2007.

"Paranoia" is notable among tabletop games for being more competitive than co-operative, with players encouraged to betray one another for their own interests, as well as for keeping a light-hearted, tongue in cheek tone despite its dystopian setting.

Ernest Gary Gygax ( ) (July 27, 1938 – March 4, 2008) was an American game designer and author best known for co-creating the pioneering role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") with Dave Arneson.
Gygax has been described as the father of "D&D".
Don't Give Up the Ship is a set of rules for conducting Napoleonic era naval wargames.
The game was published by Guidon Games in 1972 and republished by TSR, Inc. in 1975.
It was the first collaboration between Dave Arneson and Gary Gygax, the co-creators of "Dungeons & Dragons".
"D&D" fans may also recognize the name of contributor Mike Carr, who edited the rules and researched the historical single ship actions that are included as game scenarios.
Greyhawk, also known as the World of Greyhawk, is a fictional world designed as a campaign setting for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy roleplaying game.

Although not the first campaign world developed for "Dungeons & Dragons"—Dave Arneson's "Blackmoor" campaign predated it by over a year — the world of Greyhawk was the setting most closely identified with the development of the game from 1972 until 2008.

The world itself started as a simple dungeon under a castle designed by Gary Gygax for the amusement of his children and friends, but it rapidly expanded to include not only a complex multi-layered dungeon environment, but also the nearby city of Greyhawk, and eventually an entire world.

In addition to the campaign world, which was published in several editions over twenty years, Greyhawk was also used as the setting for many adventures published in support of the game, as well as for RPGA's massively shared "Living Greyhawk" campaign from 2000–2008.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: Dungeons & Dragons | Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).  The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.  It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.  "D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.

Dungeons & Dragons (1974) | The original Dungeons & Dragons (commonly abbreviated "D&D") boxed set by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson was published by TSR, Inc. in 1974.  It initially included the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.  Its product designation was TSR 2002.

Player's Handbook | The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").  It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.  Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".  Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Don't Give Up the Ship! | Don't Give Up the Ship is a set of rules for conducting Napoleonic era naval wargames.  The game was published by Guidon Games in 1972 and republished by TSR, Inc. in 1975.  It was the first collaboration between Dave Arneson and Gary Gygax, the co-creators of "Dungeons & Dragons".  "D&D" fans may also recognize the name of contributor Mike Carr, who edited the rules and researched the historical single ship actions that are included as game scenarios.

Gary Gygax | Ernest Gary Gygax ( ) (July 27, 1938 – March 4, 2008) was an American game designer and author best known for co-creating the pioneering role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") with Dave Arneson.  Gygax has been described as the father of "D&D".

Blackmoor (supplement) | Blackmoor is a supplementary rulebook (product designation TSR 2004) of the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game written by Dave Arneson (with a foreword by Gary Gygax).

Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set | The Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set is a set of rulebooks for the "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") fantasy role-playing game.  First published in 1977, it saw a handful of revisions and reprintings.  The first edition was written by J. Eric Holmes based on Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson's original work.  Later editions were edited by Tom Moldvay, Dave Cook, and Frank Mentzer.
response.summary_1:
- Passage facts: "Dungeons & Dragons" is the fantasy tabletop role‑playing game "originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson." The Player's Handbook is "a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game 'Dungeons & Dragons'" and "does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game."  
- Bridge / missing verification: identify which company published that game (i.e., the publisher of Dungeons & Dragons). Passages contain two publisher attributions that the next hop should confirm/choose between: the game was "first published ... by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR)" and the game "has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro)".  
- Retrieval direction / anchors: search for publisher attribution for the Dungeons & Dragons original/Player's Handbook edition using anchors: "originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson," "Player's Handbook ... does not contain the complete set of rules," "first published ... by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR)," and "has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro)."
response.hop2_query: Dungeons & Dragons publisher Tactical Studies Rules TSR Wizards of the Coast Hasbro
hop2_docs: Dungeons & Dragons | Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).  The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.  It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.  "D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.

Sword and Crown | Sword and Crown is an official game adventure of the Birthight Campaign Setting in the "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" game.  It was published in 1995 by TSR, Inc. (now Wizards of the Coast, a subsidiary of Hasbro).  The module is for characters of levels 5-7.

Return of the Eight | Return of the Eight is an adventure module for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy roleplaying game, set in the game's "World of Greyhawk" campaign setting.  The module was published by Wizards of the Coast in 1998 under its recently acquired "TSR" imprint for the second edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" rules.

List of magicians in fantasy | The role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" by TSR, Inc., later bought out by Wizards of the Coast and then Hasbro, has several settings in which novels have been written.  See also .

Against the Giants | Against the Giants is an adventure module written by Gary Gygax and published by TSR in 1981 for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.  It combines the G series of modules previously published in 1978: "Steading of the Hill Giant Chief", "Glacial Rift of the Frost Giant Jarl", and "Hall of the Fire Giant King".  All three were produced for use with the 1st edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" rules.  In 1999, to recognize the 25th anniversary of TSR, the company released an updated version, "".  Later in 1999, Wizards of the Coast published a novelization of "Against the Giants" by Ru Emerson.

Cavaliers and Roundheads | Cavaliers and Roundheads is a set of rules for English Civil War miniature wargaming.  It was written by Gary Gygax and Jeff Perren and published by Tactical Studies Rules (later TSR, Inc.) in 1973.  The unassuming booklet was the first product released by the company better known for "Dungeons and Dragons".

Dungeons & Dragons Rules Cyclopedia | The Dungeons & Dragons Rules Cyclopedia is a 1991 book published by TSR, Inc., as a continuation of the basic edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game, which ran concurrently with "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons".  Its product designation was TSR 1071.
response.summary_2: The game is Dungeons & Dragons; it was first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR) and has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997 (passage 1).
